pathophysiology_summary: Acute pharyngitis is inflammation of the pharyngeal mucosa; most cases are viral (rhinovirus, adenovirus, coronavirus, influenza, EBV). The presence of cough, coryza and conjunctivitis and the absence of tonsillar exudate/petechiae point away from group A streptococcus (low Centor/FeverPAIN score), so antibiotics are not indicated. Severe odynophagia can curtail oral intake and cause dehydration; the pragmatic ground-truth strategy is adequate analgesia timed before drinking so hydration is maintained and an ED presentation for IV fluids is avoided. The reasoning task is (1) viral vs streptococcal discrimination, (2) exclusion of peritonsillar abscess (trismus, hot-potato voice, uvular deviation, unilateral swelling, drooling) and deep-neck infection, and (3) assessment of dehydration severity.

differential.evidence_basis: Sore throat with cough, coryza and conjunctivitis is a viral pattern.
Must be considered but cough/coryza/conjunctivitis and no exudate make GAS unlikely (low Centor).
Severe unilateral odynophagia can herald quinsy — screen for trismus, hot-potato voice, uvular deviation.
Consider if prolonged, with posterior cervical nodes and marked tonsillar hypertrophy; less likely acutely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Viral pharyngitis leads; GAS should be assessed with a clinical score, and peritonsillar abscess actively excluded.

differential.evidence_basis: No trismus, hot-potato voice, uvular deviation or drooling — peritonsillar abscess excluded. Viral features present; GAS unlikely. Main issue is dehydration risk.
Abscess red flags absent; remains escalation target if they develop.
Cough/coryza/conjunctivitis + no exudate → low Centor; antibiotics not indicated.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative abscess screen, viral pharyngitis with dehydration risk leads; manage analgesia-before-fluids and safety-net for dehydration/abscess.

differential.diagnosis: Acute viral pharyngitis with mild dehydration risk
differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; ED only if oral rehydration fails or abscess/severe dehydration features develop.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is viral pharyngitis managed with analgesia to enable oral rehydration.

red_flags.description: Peritonsillar abscess features — trismus, hot-potato voice, uvular deviation, unilateral swelling, drooling
red_flags.clinical_significance: Quinsy/deep-neck infection can obstruct the airway and needs urgent ENT drainage.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to airway compromise or deep-neck/mediastinal spread.

red_flags.description: Clinical dehydration — no oral intake, reduced urine output, lightheadedness/postural symptoms
red_flags.clinical_significance: Inability to maintain oral fluids may require ED assessment for IV rehydration.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Prescribing antibiotics for a clearly viral pharyngitis (cough/coryza/conjunctivitis, no exudate, low Centor).
Not screening for trismus/hot-potato voice/uvular deviation in severe odynophagia.
Failing to give adequate, timed analgesia — the mechanism by which oral rehydration (and ED avoidance) is achieved.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Oropharyngeal examination for exudate/petechiae/uvular deviation/tonsillar asymmetry
Assessment of mucous-membrane moisture / capillary refill for hydration
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Home analgesia-before-fluids trial; ED if oral rehydration fails, or peritonsillar-abscess / severe-dehydration features develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Missing quinsy in severe unilateral odynophagia, or under-estimating dehydration in a patient living alone who cannot drink.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Most acute pharyngitis is viral; use analgesia and supportive care. Antibiotics only for likely streptococcal infection (high Centor/FeverPAIN) or high-risk groups. Cough/coryza/conjunctivitis indicate a viral cause.
Centor/FeverPAIN scoring stratifies GAS likelihood; low scores do not warrant antibiotics.
